findings.title: FormData file parts are copied twice (read_file buffer cloned into joiner, then freed)
findings.problem: When serializing a `DOMFormData` with a file-backed Blob entry, `node_fs.read_file` produces an owned `StringOrBuffer::Buffer`. The code then does `joiner.push_cloned(result.slice())`, which allocates a second buffer of the same size and memcpys (`StringJoiner::push_cloned` = `Box::from(data)`, /workspace/bun/src/bun_core/string/StringJoiner.rs:71-76), and immediately afterwards `buf.destroy()` frees the original. For every file entry this doubles peak memory and adds a full-file memcpy; this runs once per File field on every `fetch(body: FormData)` / `new Response(formData)` that contains file paths.
findings.evidence: Blob.rs:4085-4093 `Ok(mut result) => { joiner.push_cloned(result.slice()); if let StringOrBuffer::Buffer(buf) = &mut result { buf.destroy(); } }` — the comment explicitly notes Drop is a no-op for Buffer, confirming `result` owns a heap buffer that is discarded after the clone.
findings.proposed_fix: Extract the owned allocation from `StringOrBuffer::Buffer` and hand it to `joiner.push_owned(...)` instead of `push_cloned`. `StringJoiner::push_owned(Box<[u8]>)` already exists and avoids the copy; only the Buffer→Box<[u8]> move helper (or an `into_boxed_slice()` on the NodeFS Buffer type) is needed.

findings.title: Command-substitution stdout buffer is .clone()'d instead of moved
findings.problem: When a `$(...)` command substitution finishes, `child_done` does `buffered_stdout_mut().clone()` to obtain the captured stdout, producing a full heap copy of the buffer. The source buffer lives in the child Script's duped `ShellExecEnv` (created at line 217 with `ShellExecEnvKind::CmdSubst` + `OutKind::Pipe`, so always `Bufio::Owned`) and is unconditionally freed a few lines later by `interp.deinit_node(child)` (line 637). The clone is therefore a pure extra allocation + memcpy of the entire captured output; for `$(cat bigfile)` or any substitution producing megabytes, peak memory and CPU roughly double for that step.
findings.evidence: Expansion.rs:602-609: `let stdout = unsafe { interp.as_script_mut(child).base.shell_mut().buffered_stdout_mut() }.clone();` followed by Expansion.rs:637 `interp.deinit_node(child);`. The buffer is consumed by `post_subshell_expansion(me, stdout)` which takes `mut stdout: Vec<u8>` by value (line 545), or by `extend_from_slice(&stdout[..hi])` in the quoted branch — neither needs the original to survive. interpreter.rs:1937-1940 confirms `CmdSubst` + `OutKind::Pipe` yields `Bufio::Owned`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `.clone()` with `core::mem::take(...)` on the `&mut Vec<u8>`. Because the CmdSubst env is always `Bufio::Owned` for stdout (stdout is forced to `OutKind::Pipe` at Expansion.rs:211), taking leaves an empty Vec in an env that is about to be dropped. Optionally add a `ShellExecEnv::take_buffered_stdout()` helper (or a `debug_assert!(matches!(_buffered_stdout, Bufio::Owned(_)))`) to encode that invariant.

findings.title: MySQL result rows heap-allocate a fresh Box<[SQLDataCell]> per row
findings.problem: Both Row::decode_text (line 197) and Row::decode_binary (line 257) do `vec![SQLDataCell::null(); self.columns.len()].into_boxed_slice()` on every row. The caller JSMySQLConnection::on_result_row (JSMySQLConnection.rs:832) builds a fresh Row with `values: Box::default()` and then decode() allocates this Box, all per-row. For a result set of N rows this is N heap alloc+free pairs plus N*cols zeroing, where the buffer size is fixed by the statement's column count and could be reused across rows.
findings.evidence: ResultSet.rs:197 `let cells = vec![SQLDataCell::null(); self.columns.len()].into_boxed_slice();` and identically at line 257; invoked from the per-row path MySQLConnection.rs:1537 `.on_result_row(request, statement, reader)?` -> JSMySQLConnection.rs:832-841 which constructs a new `ResultSet::Row { values: Box::default(), .. }` and calls `row.decode(reader)`. Contrast with the Postgres DataRow path (PostgresSQLConnection.rs:2430) which uses a 70-slot stack array for the common case.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the Postgres approach: use a fixed stack array (e.g. `[SQLDataCell::default(); 70]`) inside on_result_row for column counts below JSObject::max_inline_capacity(), and only heap-allocate when columns exceed that. Alternatively, keep a reusable `Vec<SQLDataCell>` on the MySQLStatement (sized once at header time) and `clear()`/re-fill it per row instead of allocating a new Box each row.

findings.title: O(events × watchlist) nested path-compare loop on Windows (acknowledged TODO)
findings.problem: For every `FILE_NOTIFY_INFORMATION` event, the code iterates the entire watchlist (`for item_idx in 0..n_items`) and calls `is_parent_or_equal(path, eventpath)` on each entry. This is O(events × watched_items × path_len) per `watch_loop_cycle`. The TODO comment at line 437 explicitly acknowledges this needs a better search structure. For projects with thousands of watched files, a burst of events (common on save-all / git checkout) degrades quadratically.
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a `HashMap<HashType, WatchItemIndex>` keyed on the path hash (already computed/stored as `WatchItem.hash`) and look up the exact path first; only fall back to a directory-prefix scan when no exact match. Alternatively keep the file_path column sorted and binary-search a prefix range, as the TODO suggests.

findings.title: Per-assertion Vec<u8> heap allocation in Expect::get_value() that is discarded on the happy path
findings.problem: Expect::get_value() is the shared prelude for nearly every matcher (reached via matcher_prelude() from toBe, toEqual, toStrictEqual, toContain, toHaveLength, toMatch, toMatchObject, toThrow, run_string_affix_matcher, contain_matcher, etc.). On every single assertion it unconditionally calls Output::pretty_fmt_rt(matcher_params_fmt, ...), which internally runs pretty_fmt_runtime() and allocates a fresh Vec<u8> (Vec::with_capacity(fmt.len()*4), ~72 bytes for the common "<green>expected<r>" literal). The rendered buffer is passed into process_promise() as `impl fmt::Display`, but process_promise() only touches it inside the `Promise::Resolves | Promise::Rejects` failure branches; for the overwhelmingly common case (no .resolves/.rejects, `_ => Ok(value)`) the PrettyBuf is never read and is simply dropped. This is a heap allocation + byte-scan per passing assertion.
findings.evidence: expect.rs:378: `let matcher_params = Output::pretty_fmt_rt(matcher_params_fmt, Output::enable_ansi_colors_stderr());` feeding into `Self::process_promise(self.custom_label.clone(), self.flags.get(), global_this, value, ..., matcher_params, false)`. process_promise() at expect.rs:402-479 only uses matcher_params inside `throw_pretty_matcher_error` calls in the resolves/rejects mismatch branches; the final `_ => Ok(value)` arm drops it unused. bun_core/output.rs:2069 `pretty_fmt_runtime` starts with `let mut out = Vec::with_capacity(fmt.len() * 4);`. Call sites passing non-empty templates include toBe.rs:15, toEqual.rs:14, toStrictEqual.rs:14, toContain.rs:17, toHaveLength.rs:12, toMatch.rs:12, toMatchObject.rs:11, toThrow.rs:63, expect.rs:2068 (run_string_affix_matcher), expect.rs:2197 (contain_matcher).
findings.proposed_fix: Defer the ANSI rewrite: pass the raw `matcher_params_fmt: &'static str` through to process_promise() and only call Output::pretty_fmt_rt inside the three error branches that actually format it (the throw_pretty_matcher_error calls). Alternatively, memoize like get_signature() does (there are only ~4 distinct literal values), or wrap it in a lazy Display adapter that calls pretty_fmt_rt on first use. Any of these makes the passing-assertion path allocation-free.

findings.title: Pattern.cacheKey() rebuilds suffix string via += loop on every call, never memoized
findings.problem: cacheKey(index) concatenates this.#globStrings[index..] with '/' using `key += ...` in a loop every time it is called. #globStrings is immutable after construction and shared across all Pattern.child() instances, so the same (pattern,index) pair is recomputed many times. It is called from cache.add() (line 289, once per index per path) and cache.seen() (line 294, invoked twice per (child × index) at line 640 and again at lines 697/700/704/708). For a glob over a large tree this is O(entries × indexes × patternSegments) string work with repeated reallocs from `+=`.
findings.evidence: Lines 353-362: `let key = ""; for (let i = index; i < this.#globStrings.length; i++) { key += this.#globStrings[i]; if (i !== this.#globStrings.length - 1) key += "/"; }`. Callers: line 289 `cache.add(pattern.cacheKey(index))` inside a for-of, and line 294 `this.#cache.get(path)?.has(pattern.cacheKey(index))` called from the per-child per-index hot loop at line 640.
findings.proposed_fix: Precompute the suffix keys once: in the Pattern constructor, build `#cacheKeys = new Array(globStrings.length+1)` by walking backward (`#cacheKeys[i] = globStrings[i] + '/' + #cacheKeys[i+1]`) and have child() share the parent's array. cacheKey(index) becomes an O(1) array lookup. Alternatively use `this.#globStrings.slice(index).join('/')` with a small memo map keyed by index.

findings.title: rm -rf allocates a fresh Vec<u8> per directory entry despite stated "O(1) memory"
findings.problem: `zig_delete_tree_min_stack_size_with_kind_hint` comments "Here we must avoid recursion, in order to provide O(1) memory guarantee" (line 10110) but then does `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` inside the per-entry `'dir_it` loop. The Vec is only ever passed by reference to `dt_open_dir`/`dt_delete_file` and copied into the pre-existing stack buffer `dir_name_buf`; ownership is never required. This is one heap alloc+free per file deleted. The sibling `zig_delete_tree` at line 9930 does the same: for non-directory entries (the majority) the Vec is created, borrowed once by `dt_delete_file`, and dropped.
findings.evidence: node_fs.rs:10121 `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` inside `'dir_it: loop { ... }` starting at line 10115. Same pattern at node_fs.rs:9930 inside the per-entry loop starting at 9919, where only the `stack.push` branch (line 9938) actually needs an owned Vec.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist a reusable `let mut entry_name_buf: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` above the outer loop and replace `.to_vec()` with `entry_name_buf.clear(); entry_name_buf.extend_from_slice(entry.name.slice());`. In `zig_delete_tree` (line 9930), do the same and only `entry_name_buf.clone()` (or mem::take + re-reserve) in the single branch that pushes onto `stack`. This reduces allocations from O(total_files) to O(directories) for `fs.rm({recursive:true})`.

findings.title: Recursive readdir withFileTypes re-joins+dirnames the parent path for every entry
findings.problem: Inside the per-entry inner loop (line 6915) of `readdir_with_entries_recursive_sync`, when T::IS_DIRENT is true the code runs `join_spill([root_basename, name_to_copy])` then `dirname(joined)` then compares the result against `dirent_path_prev` — for every entry. Within one directory iteration, `dirname(join(root, basename/entry))` is always the same (the current directory's full path), so the O(path_len) join+normalize+dirname work is redundant after the first entry. The BunString allocation is correctly deduped by the comparison, but the join+dirname themselves are repeated N times per directory. Same pattern in the async variant at lines 6798-6807.
findings.evidence: node_fs.rs:6977-6990: `if T::IS_DIRENT { let joined = paths::resolve_path::join_spill(..., &[root_basename.as_bytes(), name_to_copy]); let path_u8 = paths::resolve_path::dirname(joined); if dirent_path_prev.is_empty() || dirent_path_prev.byte_slice() != path_u8 { ... } }` inside `loop { ... iterator.next() ... }` at 6915. Inputs to dirname depend only on `root_basename` + `basename_bytes`, which are fixed for the whole inner loop.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute the dirent parent path once per outer-loop directory (before the `loop` at 6915): `let path_u8 = if is_root { root_basename } else { join_spill(&mut dirent_spill, &[root_basename, basename_bytes]) };` and build `dirent_path_prev` from it there. Drop the per-entry join/dirname/compare. Apply the same hoist in `readdir_with_entries_recursive_async` (lines 6798-6807).

findings.title: BlockList addAddress/addRange/addSubnet use Vec::insert(0) — O(n²) to build N rules
findings.problem: Each add_* host function inserts the new Rule at index 0 of a Vec<Rule> (`r.insert(0, ...)` at lines 156, 195, 243). Vec::insert(0) memmoves every existing element, so building a BlockList with N rules costs O(N²) moves. Node.js uses `std::list::push_front` (O(1)). A user loading a blocklist of a few thousand CIDRs (common use case) pays millions of element moves while holding the instance mutex.
findings.evidence: `da_rules: JsCell<Vec<Rule>>` (line 72) with `this.da_rules.with_mut(|r| r.insert(0, Rule::Addr(address)));` (line 156), same pattern at 195 and 243. `check()` (line 276) iterates front→back and returns on first match — result is order-independent (all matches return TRUE), so only the `rules` getter (line 364) exposes ordering to JS.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `r.insert(0, rule)` with `r.push(rule)` (O(1) amortized). To preserve the user-visible newest-first ordering exposed by the `rules` getter and by `check()`'s short-circuit preference, iterate `this.da_rules.get().iter().rev()` in both `check()` (line 276) and `rules()` (line 364). Alternatively keep a VecDeque and `push_front`.

findings.title: skip_chunked_framing() re-scans all request headers on every streamed chunk
findings.problem: For streaming fetch request bodies (ReadableStream), `write_request_data` is called once per chunk. It calls `self.skip_chunked_framing()` (line 2177-2181), which calls `self.request_headers.get(b"content-length")` and `self.request_headers.get(b"transfer-encoding")`. `Headers::get` is an O(headers) linear scan with a case-insensitive compare per entry (/workspace/bun/src/http_types/ETag.rs:217-227). The result never changes for the lifetime of the request, yet it is recomputed on every chunk (and again in `write_end_request` at line 2265). For uploads with many small chunks this is O(chunks × headers) wasted work inside the mutex-adjacent hot path.
findings.evidence: FetchTasklet.rs:2177-2181 `fn skip_chunked_framing(&self) -> bool { self.upgraded_connection || self.result.is_http2 || (self.request_headers.get(b"content-length").is_some() && self.request_headers.get(b"transfer-encoding").is_none()) }` — called at 2215 `if self.skip_chunked_framing() { ... }` inside `write_request_data`, which is the per-chunk sink callback (ResumableSink.rs:636 forwards every chunk here). Headers::get is a for-loop over all entries (ETag.rs:221).
findings.proposed_fix: Compute the (content-length-set ∧ !transfer-encoding-set) bit once in `FetchTasklet::get` right after `request_headers: fetch_options.headers` (around line 1895) and store it as a bool field (e.g. `has_explicit_content_length_framing`). `skip_chunked_framing` then becomes `self.upgraded_connection || self.result.is_http2 || self.has_explicit_content_length_framing` — zero header scans per chunk.

findings.title: Builtins clone/to_vec their whole output buffer before enqueue() which immediately copies it again
findings.problem: `IOWriter::enqueue(&self, child, bytelist, buf: &[u8])` does `s.buf.extend_from_slice(buf)` (IOWriter.rs:1023) — it copies the bytes into its own internal buffer and never retains the caller's slice. Yet several builtins allocate an extra full copy of their rendered output solely to satisfy borrowck before passing it as `&[u8]`: seq.rs:196-204 stores `out` into `state.buf` then `let buf = state.buf.clone()`; echo.rs:94/100 `output.clone()`; ls.rs:338/345, cp.rs:364/371, mkdir.rs:241/248, touch.rs:222/229 all do `output.slice().to_vec()`. This runs once per output chunk (per directory for `ls -R`, per copied file for `cp -v`, once per `echo`/`seq`). For `seq 1 1000000` the rendered buffer is ~6.9 MB, so this is a 6.9 MB allocation + memcpy that is dropped immediately after `enqueue` returns. The `yes` builtin already avoids this via a split-borrow (yes.rs:144-147), proving the zero-copy path works.
findings.evidence: seq.rs:196-204: `Self::state_mut(interp, cmd).buf = out; ... let buf = Self::state_mut(interp, cmd).buf.clone(); return Builtin::of_mut(interp, cmd).stdout.enqueue(child, &buf, safeguard);` (comment even says "reshaped for borrowck — clone the slice"). IOWriter.rs:1022-1029: `s.buf.extend_from_slice(buf); s.writers.push(Writer{ ... });` — no pointer to `buf` is kept. yes.rs:146-147 shows the split-borrow alternative: `let (stdout, yes) = Self::split_stdout_state(...); stdout.enqueue(child, &yes.buffer[..yes.buffer_used], safeguard)`.
findings.proposed_fix: For seq/echo: don't store the buffer in state at all (nothing in `on_io_writer_chunk` reads it); pass `&out` directly to `enqueue`. For ls/cp/mkdir/touch `write_out`: add a split-borrow helper like `Yes::split_stdout_state` (stdout and the OutputTask's `output` are in disjoint allocations) and call `stdout.enqueue(child, output.slice(), safeguard)` without `.to_vec()`. Alternatively add an `IOWriter::enqueue_owned(&self, child, bytelist, buf: Vec<u8>)` that appends-by-move so callers can hand over ownership instead of cloning.

findings.title: flatten_tokens() is O(n²) and clones each merged SmolStr
findings.problem: When two adjacent Text tokens are merged, the code calls `self.tokens[j].to_text()` (which deep-clones the SmolStr, heap-allocating if >15 bytes) purely to read its bytes, then `self.tokens.remove(j)` which is an O(n) shift. This loop runs over the whole token vector, so a run of k adjacent Text tokens costs O(k·n) element moves plus k needless SmolStr allocations. Adjacent Text runs are produced by `rollback_braces` for every unclosed brace group, so an input like `{a,b,c,...` with many commas degrades quadratically.
findings.proposed_fix: Do a single in-place two-pointer compaction (write index / read index) instead of `remove`, and read the j-token's bytes via a split borrow (e.g. `let (lo, hi) = self.tokens.split_at_mut(j)` then `lo[i].append_slice(hi[0].as_text_slice())`) so no SmolStr clone is needed. This turns the pass into O(n) with zero extra allocations.

findings.title: Brace expansion snapshots the output prefix into a fresh Vec at every expansion node
findings.problem: `expand_flat` (and `expand_nested` at lines 766/815) does `let prefix: Vec<u8> = out[out_key][..len].to_vec();` before iterating variants. That is one global-allocator Vec allocation + full prefix copy per expansion node visited, recursively. For `prefix{a,b}{c,d}{e,f}...` the prefix grows and is re-copied at every level; with the 256-group / depth-10 cap that is up to thousands of heap allocations and redundant byte copies per brace-expanded word, in the runtime-hot path called from Expansion::do_brace_expand. The comment at line 765 already flags it as a borrowck reshaping, not a design choice.
findings.proposed_fix: The prefix is only used to seed `out[new_key]` for `i > 0`, and `new_key` is always a fresh index distinct from `out_key`. Before the j=0 recursion mutates `out[out_key]`, pre-fill all `out[*out_key_counter .. *out_key_counter + variants.len()-1]` slots directly from `out[out_key][..starting_len]` using `split_at_mut` (or a raw-pointer pair) — no intermediate Vec. Alternatively thread a single reusable `&mut Vec<u8>` scratch buffer through the recursion and `clear()` it instead of allocating each call.

findings.title: HTTP/2 DATA partial-flush copies payload into a fresh Vec per chunk
findings.problem: In Stream::flush_queue (the per-stream HTTP/2 DATA send path, driven in a loop from flush_stream_queue at line 3149), when a queued frame is larger than the current flow-control/frame-size window the code does `let able_to_send = frame.slice()[0..max_size].to_vec();`. This heap-allocates and memcpy's up to ~16 KB every time a frame is split — i.e. once per partial DATA chunk on every large upload/response. The bytes already live in `frame.buffer` and are only read (`.len()`, `.as_ptr()`, `write_all(&..)`), never mutated or escaped. The sibling full-flush branch at line 1930 correctly uses a borrowed `frame.slice()` with no copy, so this is an asymmetry, not a correctness requirement.
findings.evidence: 1868: `let able_to_send = frame.slice()[0..max_size].to_vec();` followed by `frame.offset += ...`. All subsequent uses are read-only: `able_to_send.len()` (== `max_size`), `able_to_send.as_ptr()` at 1912, `writer.write_all(&able_to_send)` at 1922. The only reason a Vec is taken is that `self.get_padding(..)` at 1875 takes `&self`, conflicting with the `&mut self.data_frame_queue` borrow held via `frame`. Compare line 1930 `let frame_slice: &[u8] = frame.slice();` which avoids the copy because the frame was dequeued into a local.
findings.proposed_fix: Reshape the borrows instead of cloning: hoist `let padding = self.get_padding(max_size, max_size - 1);` and `let stream_id = self.id;` above `peek_front()`, then keep `able_to_send` as a borrowed `&frame.buffer[frame.offset as usize..frame.offset as usize + max_size]` and defer `frame.offset += max_size as u32` until after the write. Replace every `able_to_send.len()` with `max_size`. Disjoint field writes (`self.remote_used_window_size += ..`) coexist with the `self.data_frame_queue` borrow under NLL; only the method call needed hoisting.

findings.title: to_w_path_normalized does redundant pooled-buffer get + slash scan/copy; to_w_path already normalizes slashes
findings.problem: On every call, `to_w_path_normalized` (1) pulls a pooled `PathBuffer` via `path_buffer_pool::get()`, (2) runs `normalize_slashes_only` which SIMD-scans for '/' and, if found, copies the whole path + byte-loops to replace '/'→'\\', then (3) calls `to_w_path` → `to_w_path_maybe_dir`, which unconditionally runs `dangerously_convert_path_to_windows_in_place` over the converted u16 output (line 401) — a second full scan-and-replace of the same separators. The u8-space normalization and the pooled buffer are pure overhead: the u16-space normalization in `to_w_path_maybe_dir` already guarantees the output has only '\\'. The trailing-slash trim at line 221 uses `is_sep_any` (handles both '/' and '\\'), so it does not need the input pre-normalized either.
findings.evidence: string_paths.rs:208 `let mut renormalized = crate::path_buffer_pool::get();` ... :218 `let mut path_to_use = normalize_slashes_only(&mut renormalized[..], utf8, b'\\');` ... :225 `to_w_path(wbuf, path_to_use)`. Then in `to_w_path_maybe_dir` at :401: `resolve_path::dangerously_convert_path_to_windows_in_place::<u16>(&mut wbuf[..count]);`. Callers: `to_nt_path` (string_paths.rs:98,104,118,125) which is called from `bun_sys` for every Windows syscall path conversion (src/sys/lib.rs:4018,4037,4038,4059,7476), plus install/resolver paths.
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the pooled buffer and `normalize_slashes_only` entirely. Do the length bound check against `MAX_PATH_BYTES` (const) instead of `renormalized.len()`. Compute the trailing-slash-trimmed slice directly from `utf8` using `is_sep_any(utf8[utf8.len()-1])` (already separator-agnostic), then pass that to `to_w_path`. `to_w_path_maybe_dir`'s existing in-place normalization handles '/'→'\\'. Net savings per call: TLS pool get+put, one SIMD scan, and (when input has '/') a full path copy + byte-loop.

findings.title: join_sep_vec starts with Vec::new() and grows via extend_from_slice in loop (no capacity reservation)
findings.problem: `join_sep_vec` builds the joined path by starting at `Vec::new()` (capacity 0) and calling `out.extend_from_slice(this)` once per non-empty part, plus `out.push(SEP)` between parts. For two parts `[dir_path, entry_name]` where `dir_path` is long (common in glob walks over deep trees), this incurs multiple reallocation+copy cycles as the Vec grows 0→8→16→...→N. The total length is trivially precomputable from `parts`.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:238 `let mut out: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` then loop at :240-257 with `out.push(SEP)` / `out.extend_from_slice(this)`. Sole caller: `join_sep_maybe_z` (lib.rs:265), called from `GlobWalker::join` (src/glob/GlobWalker.rs:2009) once per matched glob entry when relative paths are enabled — potentially tens of thousands of calls for `**/*.js`-style patterns.
findings.proposed_fix: Precompute `cap = parts.iter().map(|p| p.len()).sum::<usize>() + parts.len()` and use `Vec::with_capacity(cap)` (or `cap + 1` if SENTINEL). This collapses all reallocations into one allocation of the exact final size.

findings.title: Per-module heap reallocation+copy of the entire symbol table on every print
findings.problem: `print_with_source_map_maybe` (the hot per-import print path used by `bun run` via `RuntimeTranspilerStore.rs:1152`, `jsc_hooks.rs:3031`, `AsyncModule.rs:1305`) converts `ast.symbols: ArenaVec<'a, Symbol>` into a fresh global-heap `Vec<Symbol>` via `.into_iter().collect()`, then boxes that into `vec![list]` inside `symbol::Map::init_with_one_list`. `Symbol` is 48 bytes (`symbol.rs:211` asserts `size_of::<Symbol>() <= 48`), so a file with 1000 symbols pays ~48 KB of heap alloc + elementwise move on every print. The common (non-minify) consumer `NoOpRenamer` only ever calls `get_const()` / `follow()` on the map — purely read-only — so the copy is unnecessary work for the dominant `bun run` case.
findings.evidence: transpiler.rs:2348-2352: `let symbols = bun_ast::symbol::Map::init_with_one_list(core::mem::replace(&mut ast.symbols, bun_alloc::ArenaVec::new_in(arena)).into_iter().collect());`. `ast/symbol.rs:620` boxes again: `Self::init_list(vec![list])`. `js_printer/renamer.rs:98-108` (`NoOpRenamer.symbols`) explains the Map is owned solely so it can be dropped, and `js_printer/lib.rs:8013` shows the non-minify path only passes it to `NoOpRenamer::init`.
findings.proposed_fix: Allow `symbol::Map` (or the renamer) to borrow a single `&'a [Symbol]` / `&'a mut [Symbol]` for the one-source case instead of requiring `Vec<Vec<Symbol>>`. For the non-minify path, `NoOpRenamer` can hold `&'a [Symbol]` directly (it never mutates); for the minify path, the few `set_must_not_be_renamed(true)` writes (lib.rs:7961-7968) are in-place and could operate on the original `ast.symbols.as_mut_slice()`. This removes one global-heap alloc + O(symbols) copy per transpiled module.

findings.title: Signature::generate stores an unused owned copy of the full SQL text
findings.problem: `Signature { query: Box::<[u8]>::from(query), .. }` heap-allocates and memcpys the entire SQL string every time a query is run, yet a repo-wide grep shows `signature.query` is never read anywhere (only the `name`, `fields`, and `prepared_statement_name` fields are used). The same dead copy exists in the MySQL variant at src/sql_jsc/mysql/protocol/Signature.rs:83. Additionally `name` (line 41-43) already begins with the full query bytes, so even if the text were needed it is redundantly stored twice. Signature::generate runs on every prepared-query execution (PostgresSQLQuery.rs:584, MySQLQuery.rs:310) including cache hits where the whole Signature is immediately dropped (PostgresSQLQuery.rs:633, MySQLQuery.rs:357).
findings.evidence: postgres/Signature.rs:116 `query: Box::<[u8]>::from(query)` and mysql/protocol/Signature.rs:83 same. `grep -rn 'signature\.query' src/sql_jsc/ src/sql/` returns no hits; the field is write-only.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the `query: Box<[u8]>` field from both Signature structs (or set it to `Box::default()`), eliminating one heap allocation + full-SQL memcpy per query execution. Longer term, restructure so cache lookups compute only the `name` key and build `fields`/`prepared_statement_name` only on a miss.

findings.title: Postgres DataRow re-zeroes cells that were just default-initialized
findings.problem: On every DataRow the handler creates `let mut stack_buf = [SQLDataCell::default(); 70]` (line 2430) or collects a heap Vec of defaults (2435-2437), then immediately runs `for c in cells.iter_mut() { *c = SQLDataCell::default(); }` (2444-2446). Both sources are already freshly default-initialized, so the loop is pure redundant work per row. When the heap path is taken (>= max_inline columns) the 70-slot stack buffer is also still initialized even though it goes unused, and the heap Vec is allocated/freed per row.
findings.evidence: PostgresSQLConnection.rs:2430 `let mut stack_buf = [DataCell::SQLDataCell::default(); 70];` ... 2435-2437 `heap_cells = (0..statement.fields.len()).map(|_| DataCell::SQLDataCell::default()).collect();` ... 2444-2446 `for c in cells.iter_mut() { *c = DataCell::SQLDataCell::default(); }` with the comment 'make sure all cells are reset' — but both buffers are brand-new defaults.
findings.proposed_fix: Remove the redundant reset loop at 2444-2446. For the heap branch, hoist the Vec<SQLDataCell> to the statement (re-use across rows of the same result set, `clear()` + resize once) so wide result sets stop allocating per row; or at minimum build `stack_buf` only in the stack branch so the wide-column path doesn't pay for both.

findings.title: Linear scan over ~138 builtin aliases when a prebuilt HashMap already exists
findings.problem: `bun_aliases_get()` iterates every entry in `BUN_ALIASES` (COMMON_ALIAS_KVS ~122 + BUN_EXTRA_ALIAS_KVS ~16 entries) and does a byte-slice compare for each, on every call. For a non-builtin specifier (the common case in `require.resolve.paths` / `Module._resolveLookupPaths`), this is ~138 comparisons. `bun_resolve_builtins` already lazily builds `BUN_ALIAS_MAP: HashMap<&[u8], Alias>` from the same tables (HardcodedModule.rs:861) and exposes `Alias::get(name, Target::Bun, Cfg)` which uses it (line 884). The linear scan is called per user-level `require.resolve.paths(...)`, `Module._resolveLookupPaths(...)` (NodeModuleModule.cpp:507, JSCommonJSModule.cpp:313) and `process.getBuiltinModule(...)` (BunProcess.cpp:4356).
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the nested loop with `bun_resolve_builtins::Alias::get(name, bun_ast::Target::Bun, Default::default())` (which already applies the same `stream_iter_alias_gated` guard and looks up `BUN_ALIAS_MAP`), or make `BUN_ALIAS_MAP` `pub` and call `.get(name).copied()` directly. O(n) → O(1) per call; the comment at line 454 already notes this as a known PERF TODO.

findings.title: tick_kqueue allocates a fresh Vec<KEvent> on every IO-thread wakeup
findings.problem: In the macOS/FreeBSD IO-thread event loop `tick_kqueue`, `let mut events_list: Vec<EventType> = Vec::with_capacity(256);` is declared inside the infinite `loop {}` (line 996). Each iteration corresponds to one kevent() wakeup, so every wakeup heap-allocates (and frees at scope end) at least 256 × sizeof(kevent64_s) ≈ 12 KB, plus an additional `events_list.reserve(pending.batch.count)` on top. The sibling `tick_epoll` (line 920) instead uses a fixed-size stack array `[EventType; 256]` with zero heap traffic. The Vec could be hoisted above the loop and `clear()`ed at the start of each iteration to reuse the allocation.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist the `events_list` declaration above `loop {` in `tick_kqueue`, then at the top of each iteration call `events_list.clear();` before `reserve(pending.batch.count)`. The Vec's capacity then grows to the high-water mark once and is reused. (Note `set_len(rc_len)` at line 1087 already writes over prior contents, so no re-zeroing is needed; `clear()` just resets len so `add_one`'s `push` starts from 0.)

findings.title: O(events × watchlist) linear wd→index scan per inotify event
findings.problem: Inside the per-event loop (`while events_processed < events.len()` at line 418, up to 128 iterations per batch), each event resolves its watchlist index via `eventlist_index.iter().position(|&x| x == event.watch_descriptor)` — a full linear scan of the watchlist-length snapshot. With N watched files and E events per cycle this is O(E×N). The snapshot Vec is already materialized once at line 403, so building a `HashMap<c_int, WatchItemIndex>` (or sorting + binary search) once per cycle would make each lookup O(1)/O(log N).
findings.proposed_fix: After taking the locked snapshot at line 403, also populate a reusable `HashMap<EventListIndex, WatchItemIndex>` (stored on `INotifyWatcher` and `.clear()`ed each cycle to avoid per-cycle allocation). Replace the `.iter().position()` with a single `map.get(&event.watch_descriptor)` lookup.

findings.title: index_of / parent lookup are O(n) → O(n²) total when registering N files
findings.problem: `index_of` linearly scans `watchlist.items_hash()`. It is called from `add_file` (line 876), `add_directory` (line 779), `remove` (line 911) and `add_file_by_path_slow` (lines 808, 842). Additionally `append_file_maybe_lock` does two more linear scans at lines 670 (`fds.iter().position(..)`) and 677 (`hashes.iter().position(..)`) to find the parent dir. Every module added to the watcher therefore costs up to 3×O(n) scans, so registering N modules at startup/rebuild is O(N²) hash-compares. No hash→index map is maintained despite hashes already being stored.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `HashMap<HashType, WatchItemIndex>` (and optionally `HashMap<Fd, WatchItemIndex>` for the dir_fd lookup) to `Watcher`, updated in `append_*_assume_capacity` and `flush_evictions`/`swap_remove`. `index_of` and the two parent-lookup scans become O(1).

findings.title: O(n^2) Vec::remove(0) inside inner loop of diff_cleanup_semantic_lossless
findings.problem: Inside diff_cleanup_semantic_lossless, the character-by-character right-shift loop calls `edit.remove(0)` and `equality_2.remove(0)` (lines 1214, 1217) on Vec<Unit> every iteration. Each remove(0) memmoves the entire remaining buffer, so for a segment of length k the loop does ~k removes at O(k) each = O(k^2). This sits inside an outer `while pointer < diffs.len()-1` loop over all diff hunks, so a large failing snapshot / toEqual diff with long equal-surrounded edits degrades quadratically. Additionally, the outer loop in diff_cleanup_merge (lines 926/936/956/991/999) does `diffs.insert(pointer, ..)` / `diffs.remove(pointer)` on the DiffList Vec, which is O(n) per operation inside an O(n) traversal — also O(n^2) on number of diff hunks.
findings.evidence: diff_match_patch.rs:1211-1234: `while !edit.is_empty() && !equality_2.is_empty() && edit[0] == equality_2[0] { equality_1.push(edit[0]); edit.remove(0); edit.push(equality_2[0]); equality_2.remove(0); ... }` — edit and equality_2 are `Vec<Unit>` built from owned clones at lines 1175-1182. The surrounding function iterates over every equal-delimited edit in the diff list.
findings.proposed_fix: Track sliding indices instead of mutating the Vecs: keep `let mut off = 0;` and compare `edit[off] == equality_2[off]`, then slice `&edit[off..]` / `&equality_2[off..]` when scoring and when snapshotting best_*. This avoids all remove(0) calls and turns the inner loop into O(k). (A VecDeque would also work but the index approach avoids the three upfront clones too.) For diff_cleanup_merge, building into a new Vec and swapping at the end would remove the O(n) insert/remove inside the traversal.

findings.title: http2 request()/respond() iterate headers 2-3x with redundant Object.keys() + toLowerCase()
findings.problem: On every HTTP/2 request and response, the same `headers` object is walked multiple times back-to-back, each walk allocating a fresh `Object.keys(headers)` array and calling `key.toLowerCase()` on every header name. `buildSensitiveNames()` (L3883-3885) does one Object.keys + toLowerCase-per-key. `assertSingleValueHeaders()` (L3850-3852) does another Object.keys + toLowerCase-per-key. The client `request()` path additionally does a third `ObjectKeys(headers)` loop at L5956. These are invoked consecutively on the same object (L3495+3498, L3606+3609, L5950+5956+5973). For a request with ~15 headers this is 2-3 throwaway key arrays and ~30 throwaway lowercased strings per request, purely for validation passes that could share one iteration.
findings.evidence: function buildSensitiveNames(headers, sensitives) { ... const keys = Object.keys(headers); for (let i = 0; i < keys.length; i++) { const lower = keys[i].toLowerCase(); ... } }  /*3848*/ function assertSingleValueHeaders(headers) { let seen = null; const keys = Object.keys(headers); for (let i = 0; i < keys.length; i++) { const lower = keys[i].toLowerCase(); ... } }  /*respond() 3606-3609*/ const sensitiveNames = buildSensitiveNames(headers, sensitives); ... if (session[kStrictSingleValueFields] !== false) assertSingleValueHeaders(headers);  /*request() 5950-5973*/ const sensitiveNames = buildSensitiveNames(headers, sensitives); ... const headerNames = ObjectKeys(headers); for (...) {...} ... if (this[kStrictSingleValueFields] !== false) assertSingleValueHeaders(headers);
findings.proposed_fix: Fold the three passes into one: compute `const keys = ObjectKeys(headers)` once, and inside a single loop compute `const lower = keys[i].toLowerCase()` once, then do the sensitive-name check (authorization/cookie), the single-value-header check, and the token/pseudo-header validation together. Return both `sensitiveNames` and perform the throw from that one loop. This removes 1-2 `Object.keys` array allocations and ~n `toLowerCase()` string allocations per request/response. (File already has `//TODO: do this in C++` at L3838, but the JS-side duplication is independently avoidable.)

findings.title: Windows process.env set trap does O(n) scan with n toUpperCase() allocations per assignment
findings.problem: On Windows, every `process.env.X = v` goes through the Proxy `set` trap, which does `!envMapList.includes(p) && !envMapList.some(x => x.toUpperCase() === k)`. The `.some` callback allocates a fresh uppercased string for every existing env-var name on every write. With ~100 env vars, each assignment costs ~100 `toUpperCase()` allocations + two linear scans. `deleteProperty` (L523) repeats the same `findIndex(x => x.toUpperCase() === k)` pattern. Tools that bulk-set env vars (dotenv, test runners, npm lifecycle scripts, cross-env, Next.js) hit this per variable, giving O(n²) total with O(n²) string allocations.
findings.evidence: set(_, p, value) { const k = String(p).toUpperCase(); ... if (!envMapList.includes(p) && !envMapList.some(x => x.toUpperCase() === k)) { envMapList.push(p); } ... }  /*L522*/ deleteProperty(_, p) { const k = String(p).toUpperCase(); const i = envMapList.findIndex(x => x.toUpperCase() === k); if (i !== -1) { envMapList.splice(i, 1); } ... }
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a parallel `Set<string>` (or `Map<upperKey, index>`) of uppercased names alongside `envMapList`. `set`/`defineProperty` become `if (!upperSet.has(k)) { envMapList.push(p); upperSet.add(k); }`; `deleteProperty` becomes `upperSet.delete(k)` plus the list splice keyed by the stored index (or a single linear scan without per-entry allocation). This makes each env write O(1) with zero per-entry string allocation.

findings.title: require.cache Proxy ownKeys() is O(cjs × esm) via array.includes in loop
findings.problem: The `ownKeys` trap for `require.cache` builds `array = [...$requireMap.$keys()]` then iterates every evaluated ESM key doing `if (!array.includes(key))`. That is a linear scan of all CJS keys for every ESM key: O(cjs × esm). In apps with thousands of modules (common for bundler/test-runner workloads), a single `Object.keys(require.cache)` / `for..in require.cache` / `{...require.cache}` costs millions of string comparisons. Tools that clear or inspect the require cache on each reload (Mocha watch, Jest-like isolators, hot-reloaders) call this repeatedly.
findings.evidence: ownKeys(_target) { var array = [...$requireMap.$keys()]; for (const key of $esmRegistryEvaluatedKeys()) { if (!array.includes(key)) { $arrayPush(array, key); } } return array; },
findings.proposed_fix: Build a `Set` from the CJS keys first and use `set.has(key)` inside the loop (O(n+m)), or dedupe via `new Set([...$requireMap.$keys(), ...$esmRegistryEvaluatedKeys()])` while preserving insertion order, then spread back to an array.

findings.title: fs.watchFile clones the path on the success path but only uses it on error
findings.problem: `watch_file` does `let path: Vec<u8> = args.path.slice().to_vec();` before calling `args.create_stat_watcher()`, but `path` is only read inside the `Err` arm (lines 8166-8185) to build the error message. Every successful watchFile call pays an unnecessary heap allocation+copy of the path.
findings.evidence: node_fs.rs:8162 `let path: Vec<u8> = args.path.slice().to_vec();` followed by `match args.create_stat_watcher() { Ok(watcher) => Ok(watcher), Err(err) => { ... uses `path` ... } }`. The clone exists because `create_stat_watcher()` consumes `args`, but it is wasted on the `Ok` path.
findings.proposed_fix: Keep the eager clone only if `create_stat_watcher` truly must consume `args` before error handling; otherwise restructure so the path bytes are captured from the watcher/error itself. Simplest correctness-preserving fix: wrap in `let path = (err case needs it);` — i.e. take a borrow-safe `Box<[u8]>` from `args.path` via `into_owned`/`mem::take` instead of copying, or defer the `to_vec()` into the `Err` arm by having `create_stat_watcher` return the path back on failure. Low impact (watchFile is not per-tick hot) but trivially avoidable allocation.

findings.title: zig_delete_tree allocates a fresh Vec<u8> per directory entry (including plain files)
findings.problem: Inside the per-entry loop of `zig_delete_tree` (the engine behind `fs.rm`/`fs.rmdir` recursive), `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` heap-allocates for every entry. For the common case (non-directory file) the Vec is only passed by reference to `dt_delete_file` and then dropped — a pure per-file heap alloc. The same pattern appears at line 10121 in `zig_delete_tree_min_stack_size_with_kind_hint`. Deleting a tree with 100k files does 100k small allocations.
findings.evidence: Line 9930: `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` inside `loop { ... stack[top_idx].iter.next() ... }`. Only the `treat_as_dir` branch (line 9938) actually needs an owned Vec (it moves it into `DeleteTreeStackItem`); the file branch at 9965 does `dt_delete_file(..., &entry_name)` then drops it.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist a reusable `let mut entry_name_buf: Vec<u8> = Vec::with_capacity(256);` outside the per-entry loop; in each iteration `entry_name_buf.clear(); entry_name_buf.extend_from_slice(entry.name.slice());`. For the directory push, move it with `std::mem::take(&mut entry_name_buf)` (next iteration re-grows). This turns per-file alloc into per-directory alloc, cutting allocations by ~90%+ for typical trees. Apply the same at line 10121.

findings.title: FdVectorIo::to_thread_safe clones an already-owned Vec<PlatformIoVec> in place
findings.problem: `pub fn to_thread_safe(&mut self) { self.buffers.value.protect(); self.buffers.buffers = self.buffers.buffers.as_slice().to_vec(); }` — `self.buffers.buffers` is already a heap-owned `Vec<PlatformIoVec>` (see `VectorArrayBuffer { pub buffers: Vec<PlatformIoVec>, .. }` at types.rs:1440, populated via `out.buffers.push(...)`). Re-assigning it to `.as_slice().to_vec()` allocates a brand-new Vec, memcpy's all iovecs, and drops the old one for no semantic effect. This runs once per async `fs.readv`/`fs.writev` call (via `into_thread_safe` at lines 711/1296).
findings.evidence: types.rs:1440 defines `buffers: Vec<PlatformIoVec>`; node_fs.rs:2841 does `self.buffers.buffers = self.buffers.buffers.as_slice().to_vec();`. No arena/borrowed-storage is involved — the Vec is created with `Vec::new()` in `VectorArrayBuffer::from_js` (types.rs:1518) and populated by `push`. Likely a vestigial port of Zig's `toOwnedSlice()` on a stack-backed ArrayList.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the line `self.buffers.buffers = self.buffers.buffers.as_slice().to_vec();`. If exact-capacity was the intent, use `self.buffers.buffers.shrink_to_fit()` instead (still avoids the copy when cap==len).

findings.title: S3 listObjects allocates a Vec for every ETag even when no unescaping is needed
findings.problem: For each `<Contents>` entry (up to 1000 per listObjects page), the ETag path calls `strings::replace_owned(input, b""", b"\"")`. `replace_owned` unconditionally calls `replacement_size` (full scan) and `Vec::with_capacity` + a second full scan/copy (/workspace/bun/src/bun_core/lib.rs:1305-1318). Line 301 then checks `output.len() != input.len()` to detect the no-op case and throws the allocation away. Real S3 responses normally emit ETags as `"hex"`, so the branch usually keeps the alloc — but for any entry without `"` (e.g. MinIO / R2 responses or already-unquoted ETags) this is a wasted allocation + two scans per object.
findings.evidence: list_objects.rs:299-306 `let output = strings::replace_owned(input, b""", b"\""); if output.len() != input.len() { etag_owned = Some(output); ... } else { etag = Some(input); }`. replace_owned at lib.rs:1309 always does `Vec::with_capacity(replacement_size(...))` regardless of whether `needle` occurs.
findings.proposed_fix: Gate on `strings::index_of(input, b""")` (single SIMD scan) and only call `replace_owned` when found; otherwise take the borrowed `etag = Some(input)` path directly. Alternatively, give `replace_owned` an early-return `input.to_vec()`/Cow::Borrowed when the first `find` misses, but the call-site fix avoids even the one allocation.

findings.title: Glob/brace results are flattened into a single buffer then re-split into per-arg Vecs
findings.problem: Glob/brace expansion produces one argv word per match. The pipeline is: (a) `ShellGlobTask` yields `Vec<Vec<u8>>`; (b) `Expansion::on_glob_walk_done` concatenates every entry into one contiguous `out.buf` and records offsets in `out.bounds` (Expansion.rs:696-701, same shape for brace at 342-347); (c) `Cmd::child_done` walks `out.bounds` and does `me.args.push(out.buf[prev..b].to_vec())` for each word. For N matches totalling M bytes this is N extra allocations and 2M extra bytes of memcpy (flatten + re-split) beyond the N allocations already paid in step (a). This is per-glob, so `rm **/*.ts` or `ls src/**` in a large tree pays it for thousands of entries.
findings.evidence: Expansion.rs:696-701: `for entry in result { ... me.out.buf.extend_from_slice(&entry); }` (flatten). Cmd.rs:404-411: `for &b in &out.bounds { me.args.push(out.buf[prev..b as usize].to_vec()); prev = b as usize; } me.args.push(out.buf[prev..].to_vec());` (re-split).
findings.proposed_fix: Carry multi-word expansion results as `Vec<Vec<u8>>` on `ExpansionOut` (or add an `enum { Single(Vec<u8>), Many(Vec<Vec<u8>>) }`) and in `Cmd::child_done` move the inner Vecs directly into `me.args` (`me.args.extend(words.into_iter())`). The flat `buf`+`bounds` encoding is only useful when the consumer wants a contiguous slice; argv explicitly wants separate allocations.

findings.title: `which` re-copies $PATH and cwd for every argument
findings.problem: `Which::next` is invoked once per argv operand (via `arg_complete` -> `next`), and each call does `Self::path_and_cwd(interp, cmd)` which performs an env-map lookup plus `s.slice().to_vec()` on `$PATH` and `shell.cwd().to_vec()` (which.rs:196-208). `$PATH` is commonly hundreds of bytes to a few KB; for `which a b c ... (N args)` this is N redundant heap allocations and copies of the same immutable strings. `Self::arg` (line 211-213) also does `arg_bytes(idx).to_vec()` even though `arg_bytes` returns a lifetime-decoupled `&'a [u8]` that is valid across interp reborrows (see Builtin.rs:475-488).
findings.evidence: which.rs:89-105: `next` is the per-arg driver and calls `path_and_cwd` on every entry. which.rs:196-209: `fn path_and_cwd(...) -> (Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>) { ... .get(EnvStr::init_slice(b"PATH")).map(|s| { let v = s.slice().to_vec(); s.deref(); v }) ...; (path, shell.cwd().to_vec()) }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Fetch PATH/cwd once in `start()` and store on the `Which` state (the MultiArgs variant) as `Vec<u8>`; reuse across the per-arg loop. Also drop the `to_vec()` in `Self::arg` and pass the `&[u8]` from `arg_bytes` straight through to `which()`/`format_args!`.

findings.title: is_interpolated_position() linearly scans js_string_ranges on every lookup
findings.problem: `is_interpolated_position` iterates all `js_string_ranges` with `.iter().any(...)`. It is called from `parse_assign` (for every Text token containing `=`) and from `if_clause_tok_at` (for every text token peeked inside an if-body). For a tagged-template shell script with N `${...}` interpolations and M text tokens this is O(M·N). The ranges are pushed in strictly increasing, non-overlapping order (line 3468 pushes `{start: self.j, end: self.j'}` and `self.j` is monotone), so the slice is already sorted.
findings.evidence:
parse.rs:1909-1913:
    fn is_interpolated_position(&self, pos: u32) -> bool {
        self.js_string_ranges
            .iter()
            .any(|r| pos >= r.start && pos < r.end)
    }
Ranges are appended only at parse.rs:3468 with monotonically increasing `self.j`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the linear scan with `partition_point`/`binary_search_by` on `r.start`, then test `pos < ranges[idx-1].end`. O(log N) per lookup with no allocation; 3-4 lines of code.

findings.title: braces::Parser::advance/prev() deep-clones every Token, heap-allocating long text segments
findings.problem: `prev()` returns `self.tokens[self.current - 1].clone()` and `advance()` returns `prev()` (or `peek().clone()`). `Token::clone()` for `Text(s)` calls `SmolStr::from_slice(s.slice())` (line 457), which allocates a fresh heap Vec whenever the segment is >15 bytes. `advance()` is invoked once per token in `parse_atom` and again (with the result discarded) inside `r#match`/`match_any`. So every long text atom in a nested brace expansion (`{src/long/path/a,src/long/path/b}`) triggers an extra heap allocation whose bytes already live in `self.tokens`.
findings.evidence:
braces.rs:1068-1069:
    fn prev(&self) -> Token {
        self.tokens[self.current - 1].clone()
    }
braces.rs:451-458 (Token::clone):
    Token::Text(s) => Token::Text(SmolStr::from_slice(s.slice()).expect("OOM cloning SmolStr"))
SmolStr::from_slice (bun_core/string/SmolStr.rs:139) allocates a Vec when len > 15.
findings.proposed_fix: Have the brace parser take `&'a mut [Token]` (its sole caller `expand()` already owns `&mut [Token]`) and in `parse_atom` do `core::mem::replace(&mut self.tokens[idx], Token::Eof)` to move the Text out instead of cloning. `r#match`/`match_any` should just bump `self.current` without constructing the returned Token at all.

findings.title: Archive glob filter keeps running glob::match on positive patterns after one already matched
findings.problem: match_glob_patterns is invoked for every archive entry during .files()/extract (lines 1191 and 1494). Inside the loop over patterns, once a positive pattern matches it sets `matches_positive = true` but continues to evaluate `glob::r#match(pattern, pathname)` for every remaining positive pattern. Negative patterns must still be checked, but re-running the matcher on further positive patterns is pure wasted work. For an archive with E entries and P positive patterns this is O(E*P) glob matches instead of O(E*(1 avg positive + negatives)).
findings.evidence: 1408-1423: `for pattern in patterns { ... } else { has_positive_patterns = true; if glob::r#match(pattern, pathname).matches() { matches_positive = true; } }` — no `!matches_positive &&` guard, no `continue`. Called per-entry at 1191 and 1494.
findings.proposed_fix: Guard the positive branch: `if !matches_positive && glob::r#match(pattern, pathname).matches() { matches_positive = true; }` (still iterate the rest so negative `!` patterns are honored). Trivial change, preserves semantics.

findings.title: Buf::append does byte-by-byte copy+branch for separator normalization with no memcpy fast-path
findings.problem: For `SEP_OPT` ∈ {Auto, Posix, Windows} (i.e. every `AutoAbsPath`/`AutoRelPath`), `Buf::append` loops `for &c in characters` writing one unit at a time with `if c.eq_ascii(b'/') || c.eq_ascii(b'\\')` per byte. On POSIX with `Auto` the target separator is '/', so a typical POSIX path segment containing only '/' separators gets every '/' "replaced" with '/' and every non-sep byte copied individually — identical output to a `copy_from_slice`, but with two branches per byte instead of memcpy. The codebase's own `normalize_slashes_only` (string_paths.rs:298) already demonstrates the correct pattern: SIMD `contains_char_t` for the undesired separator, then memcpy when absent.
findings.evidence: Path.rs:398-407: `PathSeparators::Auto | PathSeparators::Posix | PathSeparators::Windows => { for &c in characters { buf[self.len] = if c.eq_ascii(b'/') || c.eq_ascii(b'\\') { U::from_u8(PathSeparators::from_u8(SEP_OPT).char()) } else { c }; self.len += 1; } }`. Hot callers: `Path::append` → `_buf_append_input` → `Buf::append`, used per-file in install hardlink/copy loops (src/install/isolated_install/Hardlinker.rs:109,112 and FileCopier.rs:116,119 inside directory-entry iteration) and many per-package path builds in Installer.rs.
findings.proposed_fix: Before the byte loop, compute the single `undesired_slash` for the current `SEP_OPT` (e.g. Auto/Posix→'\\', Windows→'/'; the target separator never needs replacing). If `strings::contains_char_t(characters, undesired_slash)` is false, do `buf[self.len..][..characters.len()].copy_from_slice(characters); self.len += characters.len();` and return. Fall through to the existing loop only when replacement is actually needed. This turns the common case (input already has the right separator) from O(n) branchy byte writes into a single memcpy.

findings.title: Log::clone_to_with_recycled deep-clones every message then immediately overwrites the clones
findings.problem: When `recycled == true` (the only value any call site passes — parser.rs, bundler/cache.rs, ParseTask.rs, bundle_v2.rs, RuntimeTranspilerStore.rs all pass `true`), the function first does `other.msgs.extend(self.msgs.iter().map(Msg::clone))` which performs a full deep clone of every message (each `Msg::clone` allocates a notes Vec and calls `Data::clone` → `text.to_vec()` + `Location::clone` → `file.to_vec()` + `line_text.to_vec()`). Then lines 1819-1824 loop again and write `other.msgs[j] = msg.clone_with_builder(...)`, discarding every allocation from the first pass. All the work and allocations of the first `Msg::clone` loop are thrown away.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:1799-1825: `other.msgs.extend(self.msgs.iter().map(Msg::clone));` … `if recycled { … for (k, msg) in self.msgs.iter().enumerate() { other.msgs[j] = msg.clone_with_builder(…); } }`. `Msg::clone` at lib.rs:1233 and `Location::clone` at lib.rs:743 both `to_vec()` their byte buffers.
findings.proposed_fix: When `recycled` is true, skip the upfront `extend(map(Msg::clone))` and instead reserve `other.msgs` capacity and push the `clone_with_builder` result directly (or `other.msgs.resize_with(dest_start + self.msgs.len(), Msg::default)` before overwriting). This halves the per-diagnostic allocation count.

findings.title: default_number_to_minified_name allocates a fresh Vec per cross-chunk export
findings.problem: `NameMinifier::default_number_to_minified_name` does `let mut name: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` and returns it. Its only caller, `ExportRenamer::next_minified_name` (src/js_printer/renamer.rs:1101), is invoked inside `for stable_ref in stable_ref_list` in `computeCrossChunkDependencies.rs:470-486` — once per exported symbol per chunk when `minify_identifiers` is on. Each call makes a tiny heap allocation that is immediately copied into the arena (`c.arena().alloc_slice_copy(&r.next_minified_name()…)`) and dropped. The non-default sibling `number_to_minified_name` (nodes.rs:662) already takes a caller-reused `&mut Vec<u8>`; the ExportRenamer path does not use it.
findings.evidence: nodes.rs:682-699 allocates `Vec::new()` per call; renamer.rs:1101 wraps it; computeCrossChunkDependencies.rs:486 calls it inside a per-export loop and copies the result into an arena.
findings.proposed_fix: Give `ExportRenamer` a reusable `Vec<u8>` field and call `NameMinifier::number_to_minified_name(&mut self.name_buf, ..)` with `NameMinifier::DEFAULT_*` constants (or change `default_number_to_minified_name` to take `&mut Vec<u8>`), then `alloc_slice_copy` from the buffer. Same pattern `assign_names_by_frequency` (renamer.rs:437) already uses.

findings.title: Double heap allocation per JSON/TOML/YAML top-level key in `parse_data_loader`
findings.problem: Inside the per-property loop (`for i in 0..n` at line 1978) over a JSON/TOML/YAML object's top-level keys, each key goes through `bun_core::MutableString::ensure_valid_identifier(name)` which **unconditionally** returns a fresh heap `Box<[u8]>` even when the input is already a valid ASCII identifier (`MutableString.rs:186-188` does `Box::<[u8]>::from(remapped)` on the fast path). That Box is then immediately copied into the arena via `arena.alloc_slice_copy(&boxed)` and dropped. So every key pays one heap alloc+free plus one redundant arena copy. JSON keys are almost always already valid identifiers, and `name` itself is already arena-backed (from `.slice(arena)` at line 1988) and is used directly at line 2037 (`alias: bun_ast::StoreStr::new(name)`).
findings.evidence: transpiler.rs:2010-2020 vs the existing fast-path precedent in `js_printer/renamer.rs:871-917` (`is_simple_ascii_identifier` + reserved-word check is used precisely to skip `ensure_valid_identifier`'s unconditional Box allocation for the common case).
findings.proposed_fix: Before calling `ensure_valid_identifier`, test `is_simple_ascii_identifier(name) && !lexer_tables::is_strict_mode_reserved_word(name)` (same gate as `renamer.rs:909-917`); when true, use `bun_ast::StoreStr::new(name)` directly (zero allocs). Only fall back to `ensure_valid_identifier` + arena copy when mangling is actually required. This drops 2 allocations per key for typical JSON imports with many top-level keys.

findings.title: gregorian_date computes year by iterating from 1970 one year at a time
findings.problem: `gregorian_date(days)` loops `while d >= 365 + is_leap_year(y) { d -= ...; y += 1; }` starting at y=1970, so converting a timestamp near the MySQL DATETIME max (year 9999) does ~8000 iterations; a typical 2025 date does ~55. This is called from DateTime::from_unix_timestamp (line 626) for every Date/number bound as a MYSQL_TYPE_DATE/DATETIME/TIMESTAMP parameter (from_js at lines 671, 679). A closed-form civil-from-days calculation is O(1).
findings.evidence: MySQLValue.rs:839-844 `let mut y: u16 = 1970; while d >= 365 + is_leap_year(y) as i32 { d -= 365 + is_leap_year(y) as i32; y += 1; }` followed by a month loop. Each iteration also calls is_leap_year twice.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with the standard O(1) civil-date algorithm (e.g. Howard Hinnant's days_from_civil/civil_from_days), or reuse the same JSC helper used on the decode side (`gregorian_date_time_to_ms_utc` has a matching inverse in WTF). This removes the O(year-1970) loop per bound date parameter.

findings.title: Per-stack-frame heap String allocation just to probe whether NameFormatter emits
findings.problem: `print_stack_trace` (and the GitHub-Actions stack printer at line 6360) allocate a fresh `String`, run the full `NameFormatter` Display impl into it, and then only check `!probe.is_empty()` — per frame. A 50-frame error stack does 50 heap allocations + 50 redundant format passes solely to compute a boolean. Looking at `NameFormatter::fmt` (/workspace/bun/src/jsc/ZigStackFrame.rs:233-313), the formatter emits nothing only when `code_type == GLOBAL` or when the catch-all arm is hit with an empty `function_name`; every other arm writes at least one byte. That predicate is computable without any formatting.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `fn is_empty(&self) -> bool` (or `has_output`) on `NameFormatter`/`ZigStackFrame` that returns `self.code_type == ZigStackFrameCode::GLOBAL || (matches!(self.code_type, _other) && self.function_name.is_empty())` mirroring the Display arms, and call that instead at both sites (lines 5067-5072 and 6358-6363). Removes one allocation + one full format pass per frame.

findings.title: console.table: O(rows × columns²) column lookup via linear `.iter().position()`
findings.problem: `collect_row` is invoked once per table row (from the `for_each`/property-iterator loops at lines 1014/1046). Inside, for every property key of the row it does `columns[1..].iter().position(|col| col.name.eql(&col_str))` — a linear scan over all discovered columns with a WTF-string equality per comparison. For a table of R rows that share C distinct column names, this is R × C × O(C) string comparisons, i.e. O(R·C²). Large `console.table` inputs with many columns degrade quadratically.
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a `HashMap<u64 /*name hash*/, usize /*col idx*/>` (or `StringHashMap`) alongside `columns`, populated when a column is appended, and look up via the map instead of the linear `position()` scan. Brings the per-property lookup to O(1), making the whole collect pass O(R·C).

findings.title: Per-cycle heap allocation for eventlist_index snapshot
findings.problem: `this.watchlist.items_eventlist_index().to_vec()` allocates a fresh `Vec<c_int>` on every `watch_loop_cycle` invocation (i.e. every time inotify delivers events). The snapshot is needed for thread-safety (per the comment), but the buffer could be reused across cycles instead of allocated/freed each time. Under heavy watch load (e.g. the referenced `watch-many-dirs.test.ts` with >128 files) this is a hot-loop allocation.
findings.proposed_fix: Store a `Vec<EventListIndex>` (or the HashMap from finding #1) as a field on `INotifyWatcher`; inside the locked region do `buf.clear(); buf.extend_from_slice(this.watchlist.items_eventlist_index())`. Amortizes to zero allocations after the buffer reaches the watchlist high-water mark.

findings.title: Per-test × per-ancestor-hook individual Box::new heap allocations in generate_order_test
findings.problem: generate_order_test() runs once per test. For each test it walks the entire describe-parent chain twice (beforeEach, afterEach) and for every hook at every ancestor level does `bun_core::heap::into_raw(Box::new(unsafe { core::ptr::read(src) }))` — a separate global-allocator call per hook clone. For T tests nested D describes deep with H each-hooks per level this is T*D*H discrete Box allocations (plus T*D parent-chain walks). These clones are later collected into BunTest.cloned_hook_entries only so their Box headers can be freed, so a bump arena (already present: BunTest owns one and Order.rs is invoked from BunTest) could serve them with zero per-clone malloc and make cloned_hook_entries unnecessary.
findings.evidence: Order.rs:145-163 (beforeEach) and 170-183 (afterEach): `let cloned = bun_core::heap::into_raw(Box::new(unsafe { core::ptr::read(src) })); list.prepend(cloned);` inside `while let Some(p_ptr) = parent { ... for/while over p.before_each / p.after_each ... }` inside generate_order_test, which is called once per test entry from generate_order_sub (Order.rs:36). The ownership comment at 152-155 confirms these are bitwise copies whose Drop must never run — exactly the arena-friendly case.
findings.proposed_fix: Allocate the cloned ExecutionEntry copies from a bump arena owned by BunTest (e.g. a typed_arena or the existing bun_alloc::Arena) instead of individual Box::new, since they are never individually dropped (only the Box headers are reclaimed in Drop for BunTest). This turns T*D*H mallocs into a handful of arena page allocations per file and eliminates the cloned_hook_entries bookkeeping Vec. Alternatively, collect all ancestor hooks once per describe scope and reuse the slice across sibling tests instead of re-walking the parent chain per test.

findings.title: inotify IN_IGNORED handler does O(wds) linear scan per removed watch — O(N²) teardown for recursive watchers
findings.problem: `wds: Vec<i32>` (line 670) stores every inotify watch descriptor belonging to a recursive `fs.watch`. When a watched subtree is deleted, the kernel emits one IN_IGNORED per wd; for each, the handler does `w.platform.wds.iter().position(|&x| x == wd)` then `swap_remove(idx)`. With N subdirectories that is ΣO(k)=O(N²) i32 comparisons, all executed while holding `manager.mutex` (blocking every other watcher). For N=10000 dirs this is ~50M comparisons under lock.
findings.evidence: Line 670 `wds: Vec<i32>`; line 959 `if let Some(idx) = w.platform.wds.iter().position(|&x| x == wd) { w.platform.wds.swap_remove(idx); }` inside the `IN::IGNORED` branch of the per-event loop inside `thread_main`. `add_one` at line 790 pushes wds without bound for recursive watches.
findings.proposed_fix: Change `platform.wds` from `Vec<i32>` to a hash set (e.g. `bun_collections` int set / `AHashSet<i32>`): `add_one` does `wds.insert(wd)`, IN_IGNORED handler does `wds.remove(&wd)`, and `remove_watch` (line 821) iterates `wds.iter()`. This makes per-event removal O(1) and leaves `remove_watch` O(N).

findings.title: Header EntryList is cloned twice per fetch() (three live copies)
findings.problem: `FetchTasklet` owns `request_headers.entries` (the MultiArrayList of name/value StringPointer pairs). At line 2005 it clones this list to pass by value to `AsyncHTTP::init`, and `AsyncHTTP::init` then clones it again (/workspace/bun/src/http/AsyncHTTP.rs:477) so both `AsyncHTTP.request_headers` and `client.header_entries` get their own copy. Net: 3 heap copies of the same entry list per fetch(). The inline comment already calls this out ("AsyncHTTP::init clones again for the client"). Each entry is 16 bytes, so this is ~hundreds of bytes and two small allocations per request — not huge, but it is on the per-request hot path.
findings.evidence: FetchTasklet.rs:2003-2005 `// MultiArrayList owns its allocation, so clone; AsyncHTTP::init clones again for the client.  let header_entries = bun_core::handle_oom(fetch_tasklet.request_headers.entries.clone());` and AsyncHTTP.rs:475-490 `// the same headers value goes in both AsyncHTTP.request_headers and client.header_entries; MultiArrayList owns its allocation, so clone here.  headers.clone().expect("OOM"), ... request_headers: headers,`.
findings.proposed_fix: Eliminate one copy: either (a) have FetchTasklet stop keeping its own `entries` after init and read through `self.http.request_headers` in `skip_chunked_framing` (or, better, cache the two header lookups as a bool — see the first finding — at which point FetchTasklet no longer needs `request_headers.entries` at all and can `core::mem::take` it into `AsyncHTTP::init`), or (b) change `AsyncHTTP` so `client.header_entries` borrows/shares the list instead of owning a second clone.

findings.title: MatchedRoute.scriptSrc pre-allocates MAX_PATH_BYTES (≈96 KB on Windows) for a short URL string
findings.problem: get_script_src builds the public URL by writing into `String::with_capacity(MAX_PATH_BYTES)`. MAX_PATH_BYTES is 32767*3+1 ≈ 96 KB on Windows (4 KB on Linux, 1 KB on macOS). The resulting string is typically a few dozen bytes. A new MatchedRoute is created on every router.match() call (per request), so every request that reads `.scriptSrc`/`.src` triggers a 96 KB heap allocation on Windows that is immediately thrown away after `zs_to_js` copies the bytes into a JS string.
findings.evidence: 993: `let mut writer = String::with_capacity(MAX_PATH_BYTES);` with MAX_PATH_BYTES defined in src/bun_core/util.rs:736-744 as `32767 * 3 + 1` on Windows. The getter is `cache: true` in filesystem_router.classes.ts so it fires once per MatchedRoute, and each router.match() call constructs a fresh MatchedRoute (line 653).
findings.proposed_fix: Use a small initial capacity (e.g. `String::with_capacity(256)`) and let it grow in the rare long-path case, or write into a pooled `path_buffer_pool::get()` byte buffer via a `fmt::Write` adaptor and pass the used slice to `zs_to_js`.

findings.title: Rope::append walks the whole chain on every call; callers build ropes in O(n²)
findings.problem: `Rope::append` locates the tail by `while let Some(next) = …tail.next… { tail = next }` starting from `self`. `Rope` has no cached `end` pointer (unlike `EString`, which does at e.rs:1997). The INI parser builds ropes by repeatedly calling `rope_head.append(..)` in a loop (src/ini/lib.rs:1065-1079 `str_to_rope` and 1040 `commit_rope_part`), discarding `append`'s returned tail pointer each time. With up to `MAX_SECTION_ROPE_SEGMENTS = 512` segments that is ~130K pointer chases for an adversarial `.npmrc` section header — exactly the input the comment at e.rs:1262-1264 says the iterative rewrite was meant to harden against.
findings.evidence: e.rs:1269-1278 `let mut tail = StoreRef::from_bump(self); while let Some(next) = …tail.next… { tail = next; } … tail.next = rope; Ok(rope)`. ini/lib.rs:1077 `let _ = rope_head.append(Expr::init(...), ropealloc)?;` inside `while dot_idx + 1 < key.len() { … }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) add an `end: *mut Rope` field to `Rope` and update it in `append` (mirroring `EString::push` at e.rs:1995-2007), or (b) have callers thread the returned `*mut Rope` as the next `self` so each append is O(1). Option (b) needs no struct change.

findings.title: O(n·m) linear scan: `is_pending_import` called inside the import-records loop
findings.problem: `ParseResult::is_pending_import(id)` does a linear `.iter().any(|p| p.import_record_id == id)` over `pending_imports`. Its only caller, `Linker::link` (`/workspace/bun/src/bundler/linker.rs:424-428`), invokes it inside a loop over every `ast.import_records` entry when `IS_BUN && is_deferred`. That is O(import_records × pending_imports). When the AsyncModule deferred auto-install path populates `pending_imports` (which can approach the number of import records), this degrades toward quadratic per-module link work.
findings.evidence: transpiler.rs:946-952: `self.pending_imports.iter().any(|p| p.import_record_id == id)`; linker.rs:423-428: `let len = result.ast.import_records.as_slice().len(); for record_i in 0..len { ... let skip_deferred = IS_BUN && is_deferred && !result.is_pending_import(record_index); ... }`.
findings.proposed_fix: In `Linker::link`, when `is_deferred` is true, build a one-shot bitset (or `bun_collections` hash set) of `pending_imports[*].import_record_id` before the loop (O(m)), then do O(1) membership per record. Alternatively, sort `pending_imports` by `import_record_id` once and walk it in lockstep with the import-record loop.

findings.title: IPC SendQueue uses Vec with remove(0)/insert(0) on the per-message hot path
findings.problem: `SendQueue.queue` is a `Vec<SendHandle>` (line 830). `_on_write_complete` pops the head with `self.queue.remove(0)` on every completed write (lines 1270, 1305, 1310), and `insert_message` prepends with `self.queue.insert(0|1, ...)` (lines 1147/1151). Each is O(n) memmove of the remaining `SendHandle`s (each of which embeds a `StreamBuffer`, `Option<Handle>`, and `CallbackList`). When fd-carrying messages are queued (each forces a separate `SendHandle` and an ACK round-trip, defeating the last-slot coalescing in `start_message`), draining N queued messages costs O(N²) moves. `VecDeque<SendHandle>` would make `pop_front`/`push_front` O(1).
findings.evidence:
pub queue: Vec<SendHandle>,
...
let item = self.queue.remove(0);   // _on_write_complete, per completed write
...
self.queue.insert(0, message);      // insert_message, on NACK retry / ack reply
findings.proposed_fix: Change `queue: Vec<SendHandle>` to `VecDeque<SendHandle>`; replace `remove(0)` with `pop_front().unwrap()`, `insert(0, m)` with `push_front(m)`, index `[0]` with `.front()/.front_mut()`, `[len-1]` with `.back_mut()`, and `push` with `push_back`. `insert(1, m)` becomes `let f = q.pop_front().unwrap(); q.push_front(m); q.push_front(f);` (still O(1)).

findings.title: StreamBuffer::maybe_shrink reallocates on every full drain, causing grow/shrink thrashing for large writes
findings.problem: `StreamBuffer::maybe_shrink()` is invoked from `reset()` (line 1790) and from `PosixStreamingWriter::_on_write` (line 783) every time the outgoing buffer fully drains. If capacity exceeds one page (4 KB / 16 KB), it calls `truncate(page); shrink_to(page)`, which is a realloc + memcpy down to page size. A workload that repeatedly writes buffers larger than a page (e.g. 64 KB per `writer.write(...)`) therefore oscillates: extend_from_slice grows the Vec past page size, the write drains, `maybe_shrink` reallocs back down to page size, and the next write must grow again — two reallocs per large-write cycle with no hysteresis.
findings.proposed_fix: Add hysteresis: only shrink when `capacity > N * page` (e.g. N=8 or N=16) so typical write sizes stay within retained capacity, or track a high-water-mark and shrink only when the retained capacity has gone unused for several drains. Alternatively, skip `maybe_shrink()` in `_on_write` entirely and let `reset()` (called on EOF/error) handle reclamation, accepting a slightly larger steady-state footprint in exchange for eliminating per-write reallocs.

findings.title: Per-batch Vec allocation for deduped events (KEvent & Windows) — INotify already avoids it
findings.problem: `this.watch_events[0..out_len].to_vec()` heap-allocates a `Vec<WatchEvent>` on every kqueue cycle (and the same pattern at WindowsWatcher.rs:535). The comment says it's a borrowck reshape, but `process_inotify_event_batch` (INotifyWatcher.rs:538) demonstrates the allocation-free alternative: drop the `&mut` borrow, take the mutex, then re-slice `&mut this.watch_events[..n]` in place for the `on_file_update` call. The divergence means macOS/Windows allocate per event batch while Linux does not.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the INotify approach: end the existing `&mut this.watch_events` borrow (`let _ = watchevents;`), then after `this.mutex.lock_guard()` re-borrow `&mut this.watch_events[..out_len]` and pass that slice to `write_trace_events` / `on_file_update`. `write_trace_events` only needs `&self.watchlist`, so the disjoint-field borrow is fine (or inline the call as INotify does at line 540).
